text: At first sight, this looks like a spreadsheet on steroids. Do you plan to accommodate different kinds of data that require very different interaction patterns? For example geometric data (maps, construction plans, ...), images, volumetric data, sound, etc? That’s what I see as a big challenge for visual approaches.

it's mostly a data analysis and visualization tool, so I plan to add data types and representations that are used in that context, geolocation data type, currencies, units of measurement are planned. Media types will be supported but mainly to display them. At least in the short term.

<present-of-programming>I had a memory leak, it took me 4 hours to find it, chrome dev tools for memory are so low level that they only tell me that some memory of some size is being retained at some points, trying to drill down where and what is being retained was imposible to me </present-of-programming>

Hi everyone! I’m Izu. I’m a developer and product designer, currently at Mailchimp, and have had an intense fascination with interfaces and the democratization of computing for as long as I can remember.

I fully believe that to expand access to the computer’s nature as an interactive medium, we have to provide tools beyond code that enable everyone to take part, just as Photoshop did for image editing or the typewriter did for text, and I’ve been working on a project with just that in mind. However, I’ve been in a silo on this and I’m just beginning to discover all of the incredible research and resources out there around visual programming and conveying interaction beyond code.

I can’t wait to hear more about the ideas for what’s next that y’all are working on. :pray:

Thinking about projectional editors, or refactoring tools generally…I wonder if the steps they take ought to be visible during code review. We’ve done several automated refactorings recently, and we’re left putting comments in the PR saying “we used tool X”.

“The same story repeats: genuinely new ideas that could create huge value always seem so odd that almost all people in almost all organisations cannot see new possibilities. If this is true in Silicon Valley, how much more true is it in Whitehall or Washington…

If one were setting up a new party in Britain, one could incorporate some of these ideas. This would of course also require recruiting very different types of people to the norm in politics. The closed nature of Westminster/Whitehall combined with first-past-the-post means it is very hard to solve the coordination problem of how to break into this system with a new way of doing things. Even those interested in principle don’t want to commit to a 10-year (?) project that might get them blasted on the front pages. Vote Leave hacked the referendum but such opportunities are much rarer than VC-funded ‘unicorns’. On the other hand, arguably what is happening now is a once in 50 or 100 year crisis and such crises also are the waves that can be ridden to change things normally unchangeable.”

The tool I'm making (Hest) is for my coworkers first and foremost. On the plus, that means I get to spend a lot of time on it. On the minus, it means I can only pursue ideas that fit our needs, and that I can implement myself relatively quickly.

At the risk of being absurdly reductive: this sounds like another case of.. a technologist looking at a different industry / domain / culture and saying, "I'm really good at decomposing and solving problems, so you should let me decompose and solve your problems" without really bothering to understand those problems beyond a superficial level. (Yes, the irony of me saying this is not lost on me.)
